name: SSA
summary: Every value assigned exactly once, with φ-functions or block parameters at control-flow joins. The dominant IR shape for modern imperative compilers; varies by surrounding graph structure (linear blocks, sea-of-nodes, linear-with-side-tables, trace-form).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
SSA — every variable assigned exactly once, with φ-functions at join points — is the dominant IR shape for modern imperative compilers.

The distinguishing axis across implementations is what graph structure SSA sits inside: linear basic blocks (LLVM IR, Cranelift CLIF, Bril), sea-of-nodes (HotSpot C2, V8 TurboFan), linear-with-side-tables (V8 Turboshaft), or trace-form (LuaJIT). The trade-off is global-scheduling freedom (sea-of-nodes maximum, linear blocks minimum) vs cache locality and compile-time predictability (linear blocks better than graphs).

sections.title: Why compilers use SSA
sections.content:
SSA simplifies use-def chains: every value has exactly one definition, so reaching-definitions analysis is implicit in the IR rather than a separate dataflow pass. This makes many classic analyses and optimizations more direct: constant propagation, dead code elimination, sparse conditional constant propagation, global value numbering, and several register-allocation strategies all benefit from explicit value-flow structure.

Kelsey (1995) showed a formal correspondence: CPS and SSA are essentially the same — SSA's φ-functions correspond to CPS's continuation parameters, and SSA's basic blocks correspond to CPS's continuation lambdas.

sections.title: Core mechanics: φ vs block parameters
sections.content:
SSA is built around basic blocks, value definitions, and merge points at control-flow joins. Different SSA families represent merges differently:

- **Explicit phi nodes** (LLVM IR): a φ instruction at the start of a block lists `(value, predecessor block)` pairs. The instruction has no run-time semantics; it is a definitional placeholder.
- **Block parameters** (Cranelift CLIF, MLIR, Swift SIL): each block declares parameters, and branch instructions pass arguments. φ-functions are eliminated in favour of explicit dataflow at the branch.

Block parameters are widely considered cleaner — branch instructions name their arguments — but require giving up some optimization passes that exploit phi-instruction structure directly.

sections.title: Limits of SSA
sections.content:
SSA makes scalar value flow explicit, but it does not by itself solve memory effects, aliasing, heap state, or every control-flow subtlety. Real compilers still need:

- **Memory SSA** or alias analysis to reason about loads/stores.
- **Effect modeling** (LLVM `nounwind`, `nosync`, `nofree` attributes; Swift SIL effects) for reordering across calls.
- **Region-based or capability-based reasoning** for ownership and lifetime analyses (Rust MIR adds borrows on top of SSA).

This is why production compilers layer SSA inside a richer mid-level IR rather than treating SSA as the final analysis form.

sections.title: JIT and runtime relevance
sections.content:
SSA is used in both AOT compilers and JIT compilers because it gives optimization tiers a precise way to reason about value flow.

In JITs, SSA shape interacts with deoptimization metadata: each speculative operation must know how to bail to a lower tier with reconstructed program state. Sea-of-nodes JITs (HotSpot C2, V8 TurboFan, Graal) attach deopt info at every speculative node; linear-SSA JITs (Cranelift, V8 Turboshaft, V8 Maglev) attach inline deopt records to instructions. The choice of SSA graph structure shapes how that metadata is threaded.

sections.title: Running example: φ-functions and block parameters
sections.content:
The join in the `abs` example is the core SSA problem: a later value may come from one of several predecessors. With explicit φ-nodes:

```text
entry:
  c0 = icmp_slt x, 0
  br c0, neg, done
neg:
  n0 = sub 0, x
  br done
done:
  r0 = phi [n0, neg], [x, entry]
  ret r0
```

With block parameters, the same dataflow is carried by branches:

```text
entry(x):
  c0 = lt x, 0
  branch c0, neg, done(x)
neg:
  n0 = neg x
  jump done(n0)
done(r0):
  return r0
```

Both encodings express one assignment per value. The block-parameter form often gives cleaner branch-local reasoning, while φ-node form matches many classic SSA algorithms and LLVM-style literature.
